title: Chemical adherence testing-guided intervention versus standard of care in patients with hypertension who are non-adherent to antihypertensive treatment in the UK (OUTREACH): a multicentre, randomised controlled trial.
abstract: Background Biochemical urine analysis by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry analysis (ie, chemical adherence testing [CAT]) is an objective method of detecting non-adherence to antihypertensive treatment. We aimed to assess whether an intervention based on providing non-adherent patients with hypertension with information on their urine analysis results combined with a discussion of the main reasons for non-adherence (CAT-guided intervention) would lead to a cost-effective improvement in adherence, blood pressure, and urinary excretion of albumin. Methods OUTREACH was a multicentre, randomised controlled trial in 12 UK secondary or tertiary outpatient centres and primary care services. We recruited non-pregnant patients with hypertension who were older than 18 years and were on at least two antihypertensive medications. Participants who were non-adherent to antihypertensive treatment based on the results of their first urine CAT were randomly assigned (1:1) either to the intervention (discussion of the results of the urine CAT; group A) or standard of care (group B) after visit 2. The sequence of randomisation was computer-generated through an automated randomisation service (Sealed Envelope), which used minimisation as a method of allocation based on recruitment site, baseline systolic blood pressure, age, sex, number of antihypertensive medications prescribed at visit 1, the improvement in biochemical adherence to antihypertensive treatment between visits 1 and 2, and a random element to ensure the unpredictability of the assignment. The primary outcome was the mean clinic systolic blood pressure measured at visit 4. All analyses were based on the intention-to-treat principle. The trial was registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT03293147) and is completed. Findings Between Feb 4, 2019, and Feb 1, 2023, 879 patients were assessed for eligibility, 748 of whom were excluded after visit 1; 720 because they were either adherent or had unconfirmed adherence status on urine CAT. 70 adherent individuals were retained on the study for masking purposes but were not a part of the two-arm randomised component of the study. 130 non-adherent patients included in the study were randomly assigned to the intervention group (A; n=65) or the standard of care group (B; n=65). 71 (55%) participants were male and 59 (45%) were female; 57 (44%) were White, 22 (17%) were Asian or Asian British, 49 (38%) were Black, African, Caribbean, or Black British, and two (2%) were other ethnicities. The median follow-up after the intervention to visit 4 was 2·8 months (IQR 2·2-4·3). Mean clinic systolic blood pressure at visit 4 was 150·9 mm Hg (SD 24·7) in 56 participants in group A and 151·1 mm Hg (24·4) in 55 participants in group B (adjusted mean difference -5·1 mm Hg [95% CI -12·7 to 2·5]; p=0·19). 33 adverse events were reported (11 in group A and 22 in group B). 12 serious adverse events were recorded during the trial, occurring in five (8%) of 65 participants in group A and five (8%) of 65 participants in group B (two participants in group B had two serious adverse events each). Interpretation CAT-guided intervention did not show a significant effect on clinic systolic blood pressure, but the study was underpowered. Larger studies are required to better understand the effect of urine CAT-guided interventions on blood pressure. Funding British Heart Foundation, National Institute for Health and Care Research Manchester Biomedical Research Centre, Manchester Academic Health Science Centre, and Omron.

abstract_sections.BACKGROUND: Biochemical urine analysis by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry analysis (ie, chemical adherence testing [CAT]) is an objective method of detecting non-adherence to antihypertensive treatment. We aimed to assess whether an intervention based on providing non-adherent patients with hypertension with information on their urine analysis results combined with a discussion of the main reasons for non-adherence (CAT-guided intervention) would lead to a cost-effective improvement in adherence, blood pressure, and urinary excretion of albumin.
abstract_sections.METHODS: OUTREACH was a multicentre, randomised controlled trial in 12 UK secondary or tertiary outpatient centres and primary care services. We recruited non-pregnant patients with hypertension who were older than 18 years and were on at least two antihypertensive medications. Participants who were non-adherent to antihypertensive treatment based on the results of their first urine CAT were randomly assigned (1:1) either to the intervention (discussion of the results of the urine CAT; group A) or standard of care (group B) after visit 2. The sequence of randomisation was computer-generated through an automated randomisation service (Sealed Envelope), which used minimisation as a method of allocation based on recruitment site, baseline systolic blood pressure, age, sex, number of antihypertensive medications prescribed at visit 1, the improvement in biochemical adherence to antihypertensive treatment between visits 1 and 2, and a random element to ensure the unpredictability of the assignment. The primary outcome was the mean clinic systolic blood pressure measured at visit 4. All analyses were based on the intention-to-treat principle. The trial was registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT03293147) and is completed.
abstract_sections.FINDINGS: Between Feb 4, 2019, and Feb 1, 2023, 879 patients were assessed for eligibility, 748 of whom were excluded after visit 1; 720 because they were either adherent or had unconfirmed adherence status on urine CAT. 70 adherent individuals were retained on the study for masking purposes but were not a part of the two-arm randomised component of the study. 130 non-adherent patients included in the study were randomly assigned to the intervention group (A; n=65) or the standard of care group (B; n=65). 71 (55%) participants were male and 59 (45%) were female; 57 (44%) were White, 22 (17%) were Asian or Asian British, 49 (38%) were Black, African, Caribbean, or Black British, and two (2%) were other ethnicities. The median follow-up after the intervention to visit 4 was 2·8 months (IQR 2·2-4·3). Mean clinic systolic blood pressure at visit 4 was 150·9 mm Hg (SD 24·7) in 56 participants in group A and 151·1 mm Hg (24·4) in 55 participants in group B (adjusted mean difference -5·1 mm Hg [95% CI -12·7 to 2·5]; p=0·19). 33 adverse events were reported (11 in group A and 22 in group B). 12 serious adverse events were recorded during the trial, occurring in five (8%) of 65 participants in group A and five (8%) of 65 participants in group B (two participants in group B had two serious adverse events each).
abstract_sections.INTERPRETATION: CAT-guided intervention did not show a significant effect on clinic systolic blood pressure, but the study was underpowered. Larger studies are required to better understand the effect of urine CAT-guided interventions on blood pressure.
abstract_sections.FUNDING: British Heart Foundation, National Institute for Health and Care Research Manchester Biomedical Research Centre, Manchester Academic Health Science Centre, and Omron.

full_text_sections.INTRODUCTION: Introduction Antihypertensive medications have been the mainstay of cardiovascular care over the past few decades. 1 Despite the documented effectiveness of antihypertensive treatment, blood pressure control is achieved in only approximately 50% of patients who are hypertensive. 1 Non-adherence to treatment, a barrier to effective therapy, 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 is believed to be more common in patients with hypertension than other chronic disorders, possibly because of the largely asymptomatic nature of elevated blood pressure. 6 Concealed non-adherence to antihypertensive treatment leads to many expensive diagnostic tests as well as unnecessary treatment escalations, 7 putting additional economic pressure on stretched health-care systems. Research in context Evidence before this study Suboptimal adherence to prescribed treatment is one of the major threats to effective management of chronic conditions, including hypertension. Biochemical analysis of urine (via chemical adherence testing [CAT]) to detect presence or absence of blood pressure-lowering medications or their metabolites is increasingly recognised by clinical guidelines as an objective and direct method of diagnosing non-adherence to therapy. Through a literature search of publications on PubMed between Jan 1, 2010, and June 30, 2015, using the following search terms: ((“Drug Monitoring”[MeSH]) AND (“Hypertension”[MeSH] OR “blood pressure”) AND humans[MeSH] AND (adherence OR “Medication Adherence”[MeSH])) OR ((“Antihypertensive Agents”[MeSH] OR antihypertensive OR “antihypertensive agents”) AND (“Hypertension”[MeSH] OR “blood pressure”) AND (urine OR “liquid chromatography” OR LC-MS/MS) AND humans[MeSH] AND (adherence OR “Medication Adherence”[MeSH])), we found only a few observational studies reporting the association between chemical measurements of adherence and blood pressure control. There was some evidence that the degree of non-adherence to antihypertensive treatment on urine CAT correlates with the magnitude of blood pressure elevation and that repeated chemical measurements of adherence are associated with a reduction in blood pressure. To our knowledge, no randomised controlled trial on an intervention embedded with CAT in hypertension had been done when we began the study. Added value of this study To our knowledge, OUTREACH is the largest prospective, randomised controlled trial to examine the clinical and health economy benefits of a CAT-based intervention in non-adherent patients with hypertension with different levels of blood pressure control across the wide spectrum of prescribed antihypertensive medications. We did not find evidence that the intervention was effective at reducing blood pressure during follow-up and highlight potential explanations for this finding. The effect of the CAT-based intervention on adherence should be treated as an exploratory finding. Our study illustrates the importance of inclusion of mitigating strategies against common biases (eg, the Hawthorne effect and risk of contamination) in the design of randomised controlled trials on adherence. We provide an example of an easy-to-follow tool to guide interventions embedded in CAT for patients with hypertension who are non-adherent to blood pressure-lowering treatment. Implications of all the available evidence Larger, well powered studies will be required to test the effect of CAT-guided interventions on blood pressure. Together with previously published data, our findings suggest that a personalised approach to management of non-adherence to antihypertensive treatment embedded in urine CAT might lead to an improvement in therapeutic adherence and, to this end, be more effective than the usual standard of care. The pipelines necessary to diagnose and treat non-adherence might be helpful for health-care systems involved in management of hypertension, and possibly other chronic conditions. Clinicians involved in the care of patients with non-adherence should become familiar with diagnosing non-adherence and its management based on CAT. Biochemical analysis of urine by liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry analysis or chemical adherence testing (CAT; collectively referred to hereafter as urine CAT) is an objective method of detecting non-adherence. 8 This ultra-sensitive and highly specific biochemical assay detects deviations from prescribed antihypertensive treatment by measuring the presence of antihypertensive drugs or their metabolites in spot urine samples. 7 , 9 , 10 Previous observational studies showed that, on average, one in three to four patients with hypertension are non-adherent, 7 , 11 , 12 , 13 , 14 and that the degree of biochemical non-adherence to antihypertensive treatment correlates with the magnitude of blood pressure elevation. 7 This correlation is a probable key biological mechanism that explains the increased risk of cardiovascular morbidity in patients who are non-adherent to antihypertensive treatment. 15 Biochemical screening for non-adherence to antihypertensive treatment has the potential to act not only as a diagnostic test but also as a therapeutic tool. 10 , 12 , 16 , 17 Therefore, we hypothesised that providing non-adherent hypertensive patients with information on the results of their urine CAT, combined with a tailored discussion that focused on the main reasons for deviating from the prescribed antihypertensive treatment (urine CAT-guided intervention), would lead to a safe, cost-effective improvement in blood pressure control, adherence, and urinary albumin–creatinine ratio ( appendix p 13 ). We aimed to test whether a urine CAT-guided intervention is superior to standard of care in improving blood pressure control, target organ damage (assessed using urinary albumin–creatinine ratio), and biochemical adherence to antihypertensive treatment in non-adherent patients with hypertension.
full_text_sections.METHODS: Methods Study design and participants Biomarkers in Urine, Antihypertensive Treatment and Blood Pressure Control in Hypertensive Patients (OUTREACH) was a multicentre, randomised controlled trial conducted in 12 UK sites. Ten sites were secondary or tertiary care outpatient centres with expertise in management of hypertension and two were primary care services. The list of sites, lead investigators, and the number of patients recruited is in the appendix (p 2) . Although the study used a three-arm recruitment strategy, the trial was a-priori designed for a two-arm comparison (each recruiting non-adherent participants) with an additional (third) group of adherent patients recruited exclusively as a control to maintain masking of clinicians in relation to the adherence status of participants. Based on the risk assessment of the sponsor (The University of Manchester, Manchester, UK) the study did not require an independent data monitoring committee. Eligible participants were older than 18 years, had hypertension, were not pregnant, and were prescribed at least two antihypertensive medications. Patients were excluded if they had at least one of the following: admission to hospital within the previous 2 weeks, including visits to accident and emergency, for hypertension or antihypertensive medication-related issues; unwilling to consent for 7-day home-based blood pressure monitoring; self-reported pregnancy or breastfeeding; or planning to conceive within the next 6 months. Pregnancy and lactation were on the list of the exclusion criteria to ensure maternal and fetal safety, consistent with established clinical trial standards. 18 The trial consisted of a screening phase followed by five visits: (1) recruitment (baseline visit 1 at 0 weeks); (2) additional phenotyping (visit 2 at approximately 3 weeks); (3) intervention (visit 3 at approximately 6 weeks); (4) short-term outcomes (visit 4 at approximately 18 weeks); and (5) long-term outcomes (visit 5 at approximately 26–52 weeks). A schedule of procedures is shown in the appendix (p 3) . In brief, the adherence of each participant to antihypertensive treatment was established at visit 1 through urine analysis; non-adherent participants were identified as those whose urine CAT was discordant with their prescribed antihypertensive treatment (ie, their urine analysis detected fewer antihypertensives than prescribed). Individuals whose urine CAT showed the presence of all prescribed antihypertensives were classified as adherent, in line with previous studies. 7 , 11 , 12 Urine CAT was repeated at visit 2 to establish and account for the effect of awareness of participation in the trial (ie, the Hawthorne effect) on adherence. 19 Visits 4 and 5 were follow-up appointments to collect data defined as short-term and long-term outcomes. The following criteria were specified in the protocol to remove patients from the study: (1) intercurrent illness that prevents the participant from adhering to the antihypertensive treatment or attending their follow-up visits; (2) pregnancy; (3) loss of capacity to consent during the study; (4) any alterations in the participant’s condition that justify the discontinuation of study participation in the investigator’s opinion; (5) severe non-compliance with the protocol as judged by the investigator; (6) participant decision to withdraw their participation in the study; (7) end of study reached; (8) premature termination of the study; (9) loss to follow-up; and (10) death. We tested the initial acceptability of the study to patients and the projected consent rates in 38 patients from University Hospitals of Leicester (Leicester, UK). All patients perceived the study as an important project and 33 (87%) of 38 patients confirmed they would participate in the study if invited. Representatives of the Public and Patient Engagement Committee (at the National Institute for Health and Care Research Biomedical Research Centre in Leicester) confirmed that the information for patients leaflet was entirely informative. Their suggestion of a small reimbursement towards costs of travelling to appointments was accommodated in the budget. Our structural interview tool (a Brief Inventory Tool [A-BIT]) was assessed by the patients before the study, and they confirmed they would be happy to answer the questions related to their adherence to antihypertensive treatment and have the discussion on the reasons for non-adherence based on this tool. A suggestion from patients emerging from these interviews to include information on prepayment systems for those who are concerned about the costs of medications was included in the ultimate version of the tool. A patient representative served on the study’s steering committee and contributed to an independent monitoring of the conduct of the study. The study was approved by the Regional Ethics Committee (reference 17/NW/0637). All participants provided written informed consent (in English) to participate. A good command of spoken or written English was necessary to participate in the study. The trial was registered with ClinicalTrials.gov on Sept 21, 2017 ( NCT03293147 ) and is completed. Randomisation and masking Participants who were non-adherent to antihypertensive treatment based on the results of their first urine CAT were randomly assigned (1:1) either to the intervention (group A) or standard of care (group B) after visit 2. The sequence of randomisation was computer-generated through an automated digital platform ( Sealed Envelope ) providing a randomisation service for randomised controlled trials. The platform used minimisation as a method of allocation based on recruitment site, baseline systolic blood pressure (≤160 mm Hg vs >160 mm Hg), age (≤50 years vs >50 years), sex, number of antihypertensive medications prescribed at visit 1 (three or fewer vs more than three), and the improvement in biochemical adherence to antihypertensive treatment between visits 1 and 2 based on urine CAT. A random element (75% probability of allocation to the group that would minimise the imbalance) was integrated in the algorithm to ensure the unpredictability of the assignment. The process of allocation was managed centrally by Sealed Envelope (to ensure the concealment of allocations). Participants who were adherent to antihypertensive treatment based on the results of their first urine CAT were randomly assigned (1:9) using Sealed Envelope to either standard care group C (10%) or to be signed off the study (90%) after visit 1. Those assigned to group C were retained on the study so that clinicians delivering the intervention were masked to the adherence status of participants allocated to standard care. A dedicated unmasked project manager (not involved in recruitment) implemented the information received from Sealed Envelope (whether a patient was assigned to intervention group A or standard of care groups B and C) by direct electronic communication with the clinician assigned to deliver the intervention shortly before visit 3. Complete masking of trial participants and providers of care was not possible in this study because of the obvious intervention differences between the groups. Participants were initially masked to the results of urine CAT conducted at visits 1 and 2. Non-adherent patients allocated to group A were unmasked at visit 3 when the results of their urine CAT test were revealed to them as part of the urine CAT-guided intervention. The responsible clinicians were also unmasked to provide the required intervention in group A, but they were not involved in collecting clinical information necessary to define outcomes during preceding visits (ie, 1 and 2) or follow-up visits (ie, 4 and 5). Clinical research personnel remained masked to the adherence status of the patients, the results of their urine CAT, and the group allocations. Patients with hypertension in groups B and C remained masked to the results of their urine CAT throughout all stages of the study. Procedures The intervention, discussion of the results of the urine CAT, was delivered in addition to the usual standard of care at visit 3 in group A. Aligned with earlier recommendations on handling the results from urine CAT 10 and the Perceptions and Practicalities Approach, recommended by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), 20 the intervention focused on the main reasons for deviating from the prescribed antihypertensive treatment and was documented in A-BIT, which was developed before the study ( appendix pp 14–17 ). Participants randomly assigned to groups B and C received standard of care at the time of visit 3. Changes in antihypertensive treatment were permitted in all study groups and were at the discretion of physicians, who acted in line with the best clinical practice. The study recruitment was paused and reactivated four times due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Several changes were made to the study protocol after the initial pause: (1) the overall recruitment target was reduced (from 107 to 80 per group based on the revised power calculation); (2) the long-term follow-up was shortened from 12 months to 6 months (visit 5 was scheduled for week 26 ±2 weeks); (3) the timeframe for visit windows was extended to the first safe available appointment, while making efforts to keep the scheduled appointments as close as possible to the specified windows; and (4) for visits 2–5, apart from clinic visits (the preferred option), virtual, internet-facilitated (via video-link) or telephone-based visits, as well as home-based visits, were offered to attract new participants and retain the existing ones in the study. The effect of being recruited as part of cohort 1 (pre-COVID-19) versus cohort 2 (post-COVID-19) on outcomes was formally accounted for in the statistical analysis. The changes to the study protocol were approved by the sponsor and Regional Ethics Committee. The details of the amendments are listed in the appendix (pp 4–5) . At visit 1, we collected basic demographic information including date of birth, self-reported sex assigned at birth (male or female), and self-reported ethnicity using terms taken from the UK census (White; Asian or Asian British; Black, African, Caribbean, or Black British; other; mixed; and prefer not to answer). For the purpose of analyses, these were grouped into the following: White; Asian or Asian British; Black, African, Caribbean, or Black British; and other. Clinical information including known duration of hypertension and antihypertensive treatment, known cardiovascular comorbidities, details of antihypertensive medications, as well as lifestyle (eg, smoking status and average weekly alcohol consumption) and socioeconomic information was also collected. Information was gathered directly from participants via standardised questionnaires and supplemented with data from clinical files. Anthropometric measurements including height (using stadiometer), weight (using clinical scales), and waist circumference (using measuring tape) were also conducted. Urine samples were collected without strict standardisation of collection time and were aligned with the timing of clinic visits. All biochemical analyses were conducted centrally in a laboratory of the National Centre for Adherence Testing in Leicester, in line with the established protocol. 7 In brief, the analyses were undertaken using Agilent Technologies 1290 series High-Pressure Liquid Chromatograph interfaced with an Agilent Technologies 6460 Triple Quadrupole Mass Spectrometer fitted with a Jet Stream electrospray ionisation source (Agilent, Santa Clara, CA, USA). Solid phase extraction was used to concentrate and purify the urine samples. Agilent Technologies Zorbax Eclipse Plus C18 2·1 × 50 mm column was used for the high-performance liquid chromatography separation run using variable gradients of two mobile phases (Optima LC–MS grade, Fisher Scientific, Loughborough, UK). All urine samples were run
full_text_sections.RESULTS: Results The study started on Dec 18, 2018, and patients were recruited between Feb 4, 2019, and Feb 1, 2023. The first patient was randomly assigned on March 13, 2019, and the study was completed on Sept 30, 2023. 879 patients were assessed for eligibility, of whom 28 were excluded because of withdrawal from the study before randomisation, 11 due to unconfirmed adherence status, and 709 because of adherence to antihypertensive treatment (based on their first urine CAT; figure 1 ). 70 of these adherent participants were retained for follow-up visits (as group C) for the purpose of masking clinicians to the adherence status of participants at the time of visit 3, but their data were not included in the analyses of the randomised controlled trial ( figure 1 ). Figure 1 Trial profile ∗One participant was excluded after randomisation (the participant died before the randomisation, but a notification of this was received after the participant had been randomly assigned). 131 non-adherent patients were confirmed as eligible (based on their first urine CAT) and randomly assigned. One participant died before randomisation and the remaining 130 were randomly assigned to group A (n=65) or group B (n=65). 71 (55%) participants were male and 59 (45%) were female; 57 (44%) were White, 22 (17%) were Asian or Asian British, 49 (38%) were Black, African, Caribbean, or Black British, and two (2%) were other ethnicities ( table 1 ). 122 (94%) of 130 non-adherent patients, 60 (92%) of 65 in group A and 62 (95%) of 65 in group B, attended the intervention visit (visit 3; figure 1 ). Follow-up visits 4 and 5 took place at a median of 2·8 months (IQR 2·2–4·3) and 7·1 months (4·7–11·8) after visit 3 ( appendix p 7 ). 112 (86%) of 130 patients attended follow-up visit 4 and 97 (75%) attended follow-up visit 5 ( figure 1 ). None of the baseline characteristics were strongly predictive of attrition at visit 4 ( appendix pp 11–12 ). The drop-out rates at visit 4 (eight [12%] in group A and ten [15%] in group B) and visit 5 (16 [25%] in group A and 17 [26%] in group B) were similar between the groups ( figure 1 ) but greater than anticipated (7·5% for visit 4 and 15% for visit 5). Table 1 Baseline characteristics Intervention, group A (n=65) Standard of care, group B (n=65) Study cohort Pre-COVID-19 23 (35%) 20 (31%) Post-COVID-19 42 (65%) 45 (69%) Age, years 55 (44–64) 56 (43–66) Sex Male 35 (54%) 36 (55%) Female 30 (46%) 29 (45%) Ethnicity White 24 (37%) 33 (51%) Asian or Asian British 15 (23%) 7 (11%) Black, African, Caribbean, or Black British 25 (38%) 24 (37%) Other 1 (2%) 1 (2%) Age completed full-time education <20 years 38/63 (60%) 42/63 (67%) ≥20 years 25/63 (40%) 21/63 (33%) Employment Full or part time 27/64 (42%) 28/65 (43%) Not in paid employment 13/64 (20%) 13/65 (20%) Retired 24/64 (38%) 24/65 (37%) Duration of hypertension, years 13·2 (9·6) 12·4 (8·5) Number of antihypertensive drugs 4·0 (1·7) 4·0 (1·5) Drug adherence at visit 1 ∗ 52·0% (27·1) 47·1% (27·9) Adherent † at visit 2 29 (45%) 22 (34%) Other comorbidities Diabetes 19 (29%) 24 (37%) Coronary artery disease 9 (14%) 12 (18%) Stroke 9 (14%) 10 (15%) Chronic kidney disease 7 (11%) 12 (18%) BMI, kg/m 2 31·6 (6·8) 31·8 (5·7) Clinic systolic blood pressure, mm Hg 162·9 (25·7) 157·7 (19·4) Clinic diastolic blood pressure, mm Hg 100·0 (15·4) 94·6 (16·3) Home-based systolic blood pressure, mm Hg 155·0 (20·2) 149·0 (20·0) Home-based diastolic blood pressure, mm Hg 93·6 (15·0) 89·4 (15·3) Urinary albumin–creatinine ratio, mg/mmol 2·0 (0·7–9·4) 3·5 (0·8–15·8) Data are n (%), median (IQR), mean (SD), or n/N (%). All measurements are from visit 1, except if specified otherwise. There were missing data for education (n=4), employment (n=1), proportion adherence (n=1), BMI (n=11), and home-based blood pressure (n=19). ∗ Defined as percentage of prescribed antihypertensive drugs detected in urine sample. † All prescribed antihypertensive drugs detected in urine sample at visit 2. Mean baseline (visit 1) clinic systolic blood pressure was 162·9 mm Hg (SD 25·7) in group A and 157·7 mm Hg (19·4) in group B ( table 1 ; appendix p 8 ). The primary outcome of mean clinic systolic blood pressure at visit 4 was 150·9 mm Hg (SD 24·7) in 56 participants in group A and 151·1 mm Hg (24·4) in 55 participants in group B ( table 2 ). After accounting for the differences in baseline clinic systolic blood pressure and the other prespecified variables ( appendix p 8 ), there was no statistically significant difference in the adjusted clinic systolic blood pressure at visit 4 between groups A and B (–5·1 mm Hg [95% CI –12·7 to 2·5]; p=0·19; table 2 ). Table 2 Primary and secondary outcomes (adjusted analysis) Intervention, group A (n=65) Standard of care, group B (n=65) Adjusted mean difference (95% CI) ∗ p value N Mean (SD) N Mean (SD) Clinic systolic blood pressure, mm Hg Visit 4 (primary outcome) 56 150·9 (24·7) 55 151·1 (24·4) –5·1 (–12·7 to 2·5) 0·19 Visit 5 48 150·5 (23·0) 47 153·3 (22·2) –3·6 (–11·3 to 4·1) 0·36 Clinic diastolic blood pressure, mm Hg Visit 4 56 92·2 (18·1) 55 89·4 (17·5) –2·9 (–7·6 to 1·8) 0·23 Visit 5 48 91·9 (16·3) 47 90·0 (18·0) –3·2 (–8·0 to 1·6) 0·19 Home-based systolic blood pressure, mm Hg Visit 4 33 146·4 (17·6) 31 143·9 (21·7) –3·2 (–10·0 to 3·6) 0·36 Visit 5 30 146·6 (20·0) 29 147·1 (15·6) –5·7 (–13·5 to 2·0) 0·15 Home-based diastolic blood pressure, mm Hg Visit 4 33 86·8 (13·4) 31 84·7 (15·3) –3·9 (–8·0 to 0·1) 0·057 Visit 5 30 88·7 (13·2) 29 83·1 (13·9) –2·0 (–6·5 to 2·4) 0·37 Drug adherence † Visit 4 54 76·3% (34·1) 49 65·0% (34·5) 12·7% (0·9 to 24·4) 0·035 Visit 5 47 77·0% (32·9) 46 67·8% (38·1) 9·4% (–3·1 to 21·9) 0·14 Log urinary albumin–creatinine ratio ‡ Visit 4 55 1·59 (1·94) 50 1·24 (1·65) 0·11 (–0·34 to 0·56) 0·62 Visit 5 48 1·48 (1·86) 46 1·16 (1·69) 0·10 (–0·37 to 0·57) 0·68 ∗ Results from a mixed model for repeated measures adjusted for cohort (pre-COVID-19 vs post-COVID-19), age, sex, recruitment sites, number of prescribed antihypertensive medications, adherence at visit 2, and value of the corresponding outcome at visit 1 and 2. † Defined as percentage of prescribed antihypertensive drugs detected in urine sample. ‡ Model was fitted on the natural logarithm of the urinary albumin–creatinine ratio. There was a nominally significant difference in adherence between the groups at visit 4; however, the difference was not considered statistically significant in view of multiple secondary outcomes examined (adjusted mean difference 12·7% [95% CI 0·9 to 24·4]; p=0·035; table 2 ). At visit 5, there was no significant difference in adherence between the groups ( table 2 ). Other secondary outcomes (ie, clinic systolic blood pressure at visit 5, clinic diastolic blood pressure at visits 4 and 5, home-based systolic and diastolic blood pressure at visits 4 and 5, and urinary albumin–creatinine ratio) showed no significant difference between the groups ( table 2 ). We conducted sensitivity analyses under two missing-not-at-random assumptions (jump to reference and baseline mean carried forward) for clinic systolic blood pressure at visit 4. The adjusted mean difference in the primary outcome between groups A and B was similar ( appendix p 9 ). We then ran prespecified interaction tests and none of the six demographic and clinical characteristics showed evidence of a modification effect on clinic systolic blood pressure between groups A and B at visit 4 ( appendix p 10 ). There were no statistically significant differences in mean costs of health-care resource utilisation across primary and secondary care between groups A and B over the study period. Mean total health-care costs were £675 (SD 568) for group A and £918 (1324) for group B (difference –£243 [95% CI –552 to 30] after adjustment for age and sex; figure 2 ). Figure 2 Health economy analysis Mean costs of health-care resource utilisation across primary and secondary care (costs per patient). Data are unadjusted mean costs per patient (in GBP) over the study’s horizon (comparison of group A vs group B). Of 33 adverse events reported, 11 were in group A and 22 in group B ( table 3 ). 12 serious adverse events were recorded during the trial, occurring in five (8%) of 65 participants in group A and five (8%) of 65 participants in group B (two participants in group B had two serious adverse events each; table 3 ). None of the serious adverse events were related causally to the study intervention. Mild=grade 1, Moderate=grade 2, Severe=grade 3. NSTEMI=non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction. ∗ Unlikely to be related to trial procedures. † Not related to trial procedures. ‡ These two events were in the same participant. § These two events were in the same participant.
full_text_sections.DISCUSSION: Discussion We found that our urine CAT-guided intervention was not associated with a statistically significant change in clinic systolic blood pressure in non-adherent patients with hypertension who attended visit 4 (the primary outcome). A potential reason for not reaching the primary outcome is that we did not achieve the desired power—we recruited only 81% of the planned sample size. Similar to many other studies, 26 , 27 the COVID-19 pandemic had a negative effect on OUTREACH, including the planned recruitment rate, the duration of follow-up, staffing, and budgets, which ultimately led to the smaller than planned number of patients recruited and completing the outcome visits. When compared with our previous cross-sectional studies, 7 , 11 , 12 we also had a lower rate of baseline non-adherence, possibly because non-adherent patients are less likely to consent and participate. Although our drop-out rate was lower than in other recent studies on adherence, 28 it was substantial and higher than anticipated at both short-term and long-term follow-up. This is possibly due to COVID-19-imposed restrictions as the study had to be paused and reactivated several times. We should also acknowledge that the target effect size of the intervention (a 10 mm Hg reduction in systolic blood pressure corresponding to a 0·42 standardised effect size and generally considered as moderate) might have been too large to detect. However, previous cross-sectional studies 12 showed effect sizes for systolic blood pressure in excess of 10 mm Hg upon repeated urine CAT. Beyond the primary outcome, integrating urine CAT into the structured discussion with non-adherent patients showed a nominally significant signal for an improvement in adherence to antihypertensive therapy approximately 3 months after the intervention. The improvement in adherence in response to urine CAT-informed intervention was shown in our previous observational studies, 12 as well as those conducted by others. 16 Recent, small, prospective studies confirmed that complex intervention programmes combined with urine analysis 28 or structured interviews integrated with blood biochemistry 29 can be effective in improving adherence in patients with difficult to manage or resistant hypertension. Other studies showed no obvious effect of CAT on adherence in patients with uncontrolled hypertension. 26 Although our observations align with the conclusions from a majority of these studies, we should acknowledge that the statistical significance of this finding was only nominal, and we did not apply a correction for multiple testing in the analysis of our outcomes. Thus, our finding on adherence must be interpreted as exploratory in this context. Our health economic analyses did not detect a statistically significant cost saving of urine CAT-based intervention compared with standard of care. However, the current analysis captures only short-term resource utilisation benefits within the study time. Future decision-analytic modelling will explore the potential for substantial long-term cost savings and health benefits associated with sustained blood pressure control improvements, including reductions in myocardial infarctions, stroke, and other cardiovascular events that represent the primary economic burden of uncontrolled hypertension. Our study had several strengths. First, we carefully considered and accounted for the presence of the Hawthorne effect (research participation effect) that might have a direct effect on adherence because of participant awareness about the objective of the study. 30 To minimise the risk of the Hawthorne effect, participants did not have advance warning of trial participation and the consent to participate took place on the day of sharing information about the study. We also included an additional phenotyping visit (visit 2) in the protocol to quantify how the information about the study might change adherence. The improvement in adherence to antihypertensive treatment between visit 1 and visit 2 based on urine CAT was used in randomisation to ensure that a potential Hawthorne effect was well balanced between both study groups. Second, our intervention tool (A-BIT) was developed in collaboration with hypertension specialists, health psychologists, and patients with hypertension. The tool was informed by a behavioural science framework 20 and combined feedback from urine CAT results and a supportive discussion tailored to patient needs. This design takes account of motivation and ability, and addresses adherence-related perceptions (eg, beliefs about the illness and treatment and symptom experiences relative to expectations) as well as the practicalities (ie, capability and resources), as recommended by NICE. 20 , 31 The A-BIT tool provided a structured conversation guide, enriched the monitoring component of our intervention with implementation of the principles of NICE guidelines, 31 and is shared as a resource for other clinicians with this publication ( appendix pp 14–17 ). Third, we ensured that clinicians from all recruitment sites received dedicated training so that the intervention in group A was consistently applied through the results of urine CAT combined with the A-BIT. Conversely, patients in group B were managed using the usual standards of clinical care. We anticipated, given the objectives of the study, that clinicians might go beyond their normal clinical attention to adherence and over-deliver even in the absence of information of urine CAT in group B. 19 Thus, we included group C (adherent patients) in the study design to bring the delivery of the care in group B as close as possible to the usual clinical standards, in which the objective adherence status of patients is unknown and information on urine biochemistry is largely unavailable. Fourth, our measures of adherence come from direct, objective analysis of urine—a robust method to detect therapeutic non-adherence. 10 This aligns with the recommendation from a Cochrane review on non-adherence. 6 Our urine CAT analyses were conducted in one central laboratory with appropriate expertise and accreditations and covered almost all commonly prescribed antihypertensive medications or their metabolites using robust methodology ( appendix p 6 ). Finally, our study has been successful in engaging individuals from populations under-represented in research and studies on adherence—more than 50% of non-adherent patients with hypertension recruited into our trial were of non-White ethnicity. We are aware of several limitations of our analysis. Beyond the described effects of the COVID-19 pandemic, we had to adapt the study protocol to facilitate safe attendance of follow-up visits via remote appointments. We appreciate that these factors limit the generalisability and consistency of our findings. We also acknowledge that the trial was designed to capture primarily short-term effects of the intervention and that urine CAT in essence captures a snapshot of adherence rather than persistence with treatment. 8 Other techniques (eg, medication event monitoring systems) are better suited to define persistence with treatment, including how patients adhere to the treatment recommendations on a daily basis. We also appreciate the complex and aetiologically heterogeneous nature of non-adherence to antihypertensive treatment as an outcome. Although our A-BIT tool was designed with a wide range of the most common intentional and non-intentional causes of non-adherence in mind, inevitably not all patients respond to interventions embedded in urine CAT in the same way. 12 Future studies should investigate the potential drivers of blood pressure response to the intervention to enhance the development of a more personalised approach to the management of non-adherence. Those studies should also dissect the drivers for changes in adherence on follow-up; for example, to specify the extent to which they are driven by changes in prescription for antihypertensive medications. Due to these limitations, the findings from our study should be viewed as hypothesis-generating rather than definitive. Nonetheless, the insights collected from the OUTREACH study together with the existing evidence should inspire larger, well powered trials to conclusively evaluate the effect of urine CAT-based intervention on adherence and blood pressure.
full_text_sections.DATA SHARING: Data sharing Anonymised individual level data from this trial cannot be shared with external researchers for secondary use. All the necessary information is available in tables, figures, and the appendix. Reasonable requests for additional summary level data can be made to the Chief Investigator ( maciej.tomaszewski@manchester.ac.uk ).
